nativeRange.habitat: The Glowlight Rasbora is native to forest streams and slow-moving waters in Borneo and Sumatra, where conditions are soft and weakly acidic to neutral. These habitats are typically shaded, with water stained brown by tannins from leaf litter and surrounded by aquatic plant growth.

decorPreferences: Dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Cryptocoryne, Anubias, Microsorum, Taxiphyllum)
Dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration
Driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins
Open mid-water swimming space for schooling

diet.notes: Micropredator in nature; generally accepts small high-quality dried flake or micro-pellet foods as a staple. Color and condition tend to improve with regular offerings of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, baby brine shrimp, bloodworm, and micro worms.

goodWith: Other small peaceful rasboras (Trigonostigma heteromorpha, T. espei)
avoidWith: Aggressive or boisterous species that may outcompete them at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by -serwacy01-, 5184x3456. Tack-sharp lone specimen against dark background; vivid orange-red lateral stripe with the diagnostic black lambchop-shaped border shown clearly on a fully in-colour fish.

Photo by NasserHalaweh, 1212x908. Shoal of several individuals in a planted tank, showing the schooling behaviour typical of the species; slightly hazy with a green cast so kept as a supporting group shot rather than the lead image.
Photo by -serwacy01-, 1024x683. Sharp, vividly-coloured single individual in a different pose; red stripe and black border well resolved, added for angle variety.

summary: The Glowlight Rasbora (Trigonostigma hengeli) is a small, slender schooling fish from the forest streams of Borneo and Sumatra, recognized by its vivid orange-yellow lateral stripe edged in dark pigment. Generally considered beginner-friendly, it does best in groups of 8 or more in a soft-water planted aquarium.
careNotes: The Glowlight Rasbora is the smallest and slimmest of the three common Trigonostigma species — noticeably more slender-bodied than both the Harlequin Rasbora (T. heteromorpha) and the Lambchop Rasbora (T. espei). Its lateral stripe is a warm orange-yellow with a darker border, rather than the solid black triangle seen on the Harlequin. Best kept in schools of at least 8–10; smaller groups tend to be shy and noticeably less active. A well-planted aquarium with dim lighting, a dark substrate, and some tannin input from driftwood or dried catappa leaves is generally advised to bring out natural coloration and reduce stress. The species prefers soft, slightly acidic water reflecting its Bornean and Sumatran forest-stream origins, though it has been reported to adapt to neutral conditions. For comparison with the two closely related sibling species, see [harlequin-rasbora] (T. heteromorpha — larger, deeper-bodied, with a broader black triangle mark) and [lambchop-rasbora] (T. espei — intermediate in size, with orange-red coloration and a lambchop-shaped marking).
breedingNotes: Spawning tends to occur most readily in very soft, acidic conditioning water (pH around 5.5–6.5, low hardness) at slightly elevated temperature. Eggs are deposited on the undersides of broad plant leaves in a characteristic behavior shared across the genus. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming within roughly one week and initially require very fine foods (infusoria, vinegar eels, or commercial fry food) before transitioning to baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for a species-specific page; SeriouslyFish is the first listed secondary for the fish-freshwater slice and was promoted to primary. Provided size (25–30 mm), temperature (23–28°C), pH (5.0–7.5), minimum tank (54L / 60×30 cm base), breeding notes, native range (Sumatra and Borneo), and habitat description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Trigonostigma hengeli, Meinken 1956), family (Danionidae), order (Cypriniformes), native range (Sumatra and Borneo, Indonesia), max size 3.0 cm SL, temperature 23–28°C, hardness 5–12 dH. pH shown as 6.0 only (likely truncated data entry) — SeriouslyFish pH 5.0–7.5 used instead as it reflects the species' known soft-water range.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are deposited on the undersides of broad plant leaves, a behavior shared across the Trigonostigma genus. Spawning requires soft, acidic conditioning water. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours, and fry become free-swimming at around one week of age.
